def_en: A very large quantity of something; plentifulness.
def_zh: abundance of resources; abundance of time
quote_en: When you have an abundance of resources, like extra crayons or snacks, sharing them with a friend makes everyone feel richer.
quote_zh: An abundance of time on a lazy weekend lets you build a fort, read a whole book, or simply laugh with your family until your cheeks hurt.
ex_en: True abundance isn’t measured by how much you keep, but by how freely you give—a quiet truth that grows like a garden planted with care.
ex_zh: A generous heart turns a simple afternoon into a treasure, and a small kindness into a memory that warms you for years.

def_en: To gather or collect over time; to build up.
quote_en: You can accumulate wisdom by listening to your grandparents’ stories—each tale is like a coin added to a treasure chest of understanding.
quote_zh: If you accumulate too many things in your room without ever giving away what you no longer use, soon you won’t have space to play.
ex_en: The wise gardener knows that soil does not accumulate value by holding onto every seed; it gives, and in giving, it grows richer.
ex_zh: A life well lived is not measured by what we accumulate, but by what we accumulate in the hearts of others: kindness, patience, and the quiet echo of our care.

def_en: Familiar with something or someone; having knowledge or experience.
quote_en: When you move to a new school, the fastest way to become acquainted with your classmates is to smile and ask them about their favorite games.
quote_zh: At summer camp, you get acquainted with people from different towns by sharing a tent, telling jokes, and roasting marshmallows together.
ex_en: To become acquainted with your own courage, you must sometimes do the thing you fear—and find that the fear was only a shadow.
ex_zh: A mind that gets acquainted with many ideas is like a house with many windows; each one lets in a different light, and together they make the whole world visible.

def_en: Sharp or severe in effect; keenly perceptive; intense.
quote_en: An owl uses its acute hearing to catch the faintest rustle of a mouse in the grass—a superpower some animals have.
quote_zh: When you pay acute attention during math class, the numbers stop being a puzzle and start telling a story you can follow.
ex_en: An acute sense of fairness is a gift: it lets you notice when someone is left out, so you can be the one who says, “Come join us.”
ex_zh: The sharpest eyes are not those that see the most, but those that see what truly matters—a friend’s quiet sadness, a moment that needs a gentle word.

def_en: A substance added to something to improve or alter it.
def_zh: food additive; additive in a recipe
quote_en: Some snacks contain a food additive that makes them last longer on the shelf, but fresh fruit doesn’t need any help to be delicious.
quote_zh: When you bake cookies, an additive in a recipe might be a pinch of cinnamon—a small extra that turns good into amazing.
ex_en: Kindness is the additive that makes ordinary friendships extraordinary; without it, the recipe of life feels flat.
ex_zh: A smile is the sweetest additive to any conversation—it costs nothing, yet it changes everything.

def_en: Sufficient; enough; satisfactory in quality or quantity.
quote_en: Getting adequate sleep before a big test helps your brain work like a well-oiled machine.
quote_zh: In a crowded car on a family trip, “adequate space” might mean you and your sibling agree on whose elbow gets the armrest—a lesson in sharing.
ex_en: Adequate effort may get you a passing grade, but wholehearted effort builds a foundation of pride that no score can measure.
ex_zh: A life with adequate things is comfortable; a life with adequate gratitude is rich beyond counting.

def_en: One who attacks or initiates conflict without provocation.
def_zh: aggressor in a fight; aggressor on the playground
quote_en: The real aggressor in a fight is often the one who starts pushing, not the one who finally pushes back.
quote_zh: On the playground, an aggressor might try to take your ball, but you can show true strength by walking away and inviting someone else to play.
ex_en: The aggressor believes that striking first wins, but history shows that those who build bridges, not walls, leave the deeper mark.
ex_zh: True courage is not being the aggressor, but being the one who says, “Let’s find another way.”

def_en: Able to move quickly and easily; nimble; mentally quick.
quote_en: An agile climber can scramble up the jungle gym faster than anyone, swinging from bar to bar like a monkey.
quote_zh: With an agile mind, you can solve tricky riddles that leave others scratching their heads—turning “I don’t know” into “I figured it out!”
ex_en: An agile body is a gift, but an agile heart—one that bends without breaking—is a superpower that lasts a lifetime.
ex_zh: Be agile in your thinking: when one path is blocked, look for another; the world is full of doors waiting to be opened.

def_en: The repetition of initial sounds in neighboring words.
quote_en: “Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers” is a tongue‑twister full of playful alliteration that makes it fun to say.
quote_zh: Poets use alliteration in poetry to make their lines sing—like “silver sails shimmer on the sea.”
ex_en: Alliteration is the jewelry of language: a small ornament that makes a sentence sparkle.
ex_zh: When you find the right alliteration, words dance off the tongue and leave a trail of music in the air.

def_en: A diplomatic representative sent by one country to another.
quote_en: A school ambassador greets new students, shows them around, and makes sure they don’t eat lunch alone on their first day.
quote_zh: You can be an ambassador of kindness by including someone who seems lonely, speaking up when you see unfairness, and offering a hand without being asked.
ex_en: A true ambassador carries not only the words of her country, but the character of her people.
ex_zh: The greatest ambassadors are not those with the grandest titles, but those who, by their daily actions, make the world a little more welcoming.

def_en: Plentiful; more than enough; generous in quantity.
quote_en: With ample time before dinner, you can finish your homework and still build a pillow fort for story hour.
quote_zh: An ample space in your backpack means you can carry a friend’s lunch when they forget theirs, no questions asked.
ex_en: Ample resources are a blessing, but ample generosity is a legacy.
ex_zh: There is ample room in the world for every dream; the only limit is the courage to begin.

def_en: Without a known name; unidentified; nameless.
quote_en: An anonymous donor left a box of books at the school door—no name, just a note: “For everyone to enjoy.”
quote_zh: Finding an anonymous letter in your locker that says “You are awesome” can turn a gloomy day into a sunny one.
ex_en: Some of the greatest gifts come from anonymous hands—people who give without needing credit, like rain that waters the earth without asking to be seen.
ex_zh: Anonymous kindness is the purest kind: it expects nothing in return, yet it plants seeds that bloom for years.

def_en: A collectible object from an earlier era; ancient; old-fashioned.
quote_en: Browsing an antique store feels like a treasure hunt—each dusty shelf holds a story from long ago.
quote_zh: My grandmother’s antique clock chimes every hour, and its deep, steady voice makes my room feel like a castle.
ex_en: Antiques remind us that things made with care outlast their makers; we are all caretakers of what came before.
ex_zh: An antique is not old because it has been used; it is precious because it has been cherished.

def_en: Supplementary material at the end of a book; an additional section.
def_zh: appendix of a book; informational appendix
quote_en: The appendix of a book often contains maps or fun facts that the main story didn’t have room to tell.
quote_zh: An informational appendix in your science textbook explains how volcanoes work—it’s like a bonus chapter for curious minds.
ex_en: Life’s most valuable lessons are often found in the appendix: the detours, the mistakes, the quiet moments we almost skipped.
ex_zh: Don’t rush past the appendix; sometimes the best treasure is hidden where you least expect it.

def_en: A formal request; the act of putting something to use.
quote_en: Filling out a job application for your first paper route teaches you to write neatly, tell the truth, and show what makes you reliable.
quote_zh: The application of knowledge is what turns a page of facts into a tower built with your own hands.
ex_en: An application is a promise: you offer who you are, and you hope to be chosen. But the bravest applications are those you write to yourself, promising to try again.
ex_zh: Knowledge without application is a seed never planted; wisdom is what grows when you put both hands in the soil.

def_en: Close to accurate but not exact; rough estimate.
quote_en: “The approximate time for the bus to arrive is 3:15”—close enough that you don’t have to stand there forever.
quote_zh: You can guess the approximate distance to the park by counting your steps; it’s not exact, but it gives you an idea.
ex_en: In life, an approximate direction is often enough: you don’t need to see the whole path to take the first step.
ex_zh: The greatest discoveries began with an approximate guess, a “what if” whispered in the dark.

def_en: The study of human history through excavation and analysis of artifacts.
quote_en: The field of archaeology is like being a detective for people who lived thousands of years ago—you piece together their stories from broken pots and buried coins.
quote_zh: Joining an archaeology dig as a student means getting your hands dirty and maybe unearthing a shard of pottery that last touched a child’s hand long ago.
ex_en: Archaeology teaches us that every civilization leaves a mark; the question is whether we leave one of beauty or of ruin.
ex_zh: The past is not gone; it lives in the soil, waiting for patient hands to listen.

def_en: A member of the nobility; a person of privileged class.
quote_en: An aristocratic family in history might have lived in a castle, but they also had the responsibility to care for the villagers who worked their land.
quote_zh: Having aristocratic manners doesn’t mean wearing fancy clothes; it means treating everyone with respect, whether they are a king or a gardener.
ex_en: True aristocrats are not born; they are made by their choices—by kindness that does not look down, and strength that lifts others up.
ex_zh: Nobility is not in a title, but in a heart that remembers where it came from and reaches out to those still climbing.

def_en: A particular part or feature; a way of viewing something; appearance.
quote_en: One important aspect of being a good teammate is celebrating when others score, even if you were the one who passed the ball.
quote_zh: When you bake a cake, every aspect matters—the flour, the oven, the love you mix in—and together they create something wonderful.
ex_en: The most beautiful aspect of a person is often the one they least expect: their willingness to listen, their courage to be kind.
ex_zh: Look at every aspect of a problem before you decide; the solution often hides in the side you almost ignored.

def_en: To state confidently; to declare forcefully; to insist on one’s rights.
quote_en: It can be scary to assert yourself when someone cuts in line, but a calm “I was here first” shows you respect yourself.
quote_zh: In a group project, you can assert your opinion without shouting—just share your idea and say, “What do you think?” with an open mind.
ex_en: To assert yourself is not to push others down, but to stand tall so that no one can push you aside.
ex_zh: The strongest voices are not the loudest; they are the ones that speak truth with the quiet confidence of a river that cannot be turned.

def_en: A varied collection; a mixture of different things.
def_zh: assortment of colors; assortment of toys
quote_en: An assortment of colors in your crayon box lets you draw a rainbow, a sunset, or a dragon with scales of purple and gold.
quote_zh: The assortment of toys at the school fair—stuffed animals, puzzles, and bouncy balls—meant there was something special for every kid.
ex_en: Life offers an assortment of paths; the wise traveler does not fear choosing one, knowing that every road teaches something the other could not.
ex_zh: An assortment of friends is a treasure: the one who makes you laugh, the one who listens, the one who dares you to be brave.

def_en: To achieve or reach through effort; to accomplish.
def_zh: attain a goal; attain a skill
quote_en: You can attain a goal like reading fifty books in a year by reading just a few pages every day—small steps lead to big finishes.
quote_zh: To attain a skill like playing the recorder, you must practice even when the notes squeak; each wrong note is a step toward the right one.
ex_en: What you attain by striving is valuable, but what you attain by growing is priceless: patience, courage, a heart that does not quit.
ex_zh: The summit is not the only reward; the climb itself, with its scrapes and laughs, is where you become the one who can stand at the top.

def_en: Able to be heard; loud enough to perceive.
quote_en: An audible whisper in the library is just loud enough for your friend to hear your secret, but quiet enough not to disturb the readers.
quote_zh: When your mom lets out an audible sigh of relief after you finally clean your room, you know it was worth the effort.
ex_en: Some truths are not meant to be shouted; they are audible only to those who pause long enough to listen with their whole heart.
ex_zh: An audible laugh shared between friends is one of the purest sounds—proof that joy is always better when it echoes.

def_en: A trial performance to evaluate abilities.
def_zh: audition for a play; audition nerves
quote_en: Your first audition for a play might make your stomach flutter, but stepping on stage anyway is a victory no matter what the director decides.
quote_zh: Audition nerves are natural—even professional actors feel them—but they fade once you start doing what you love.
ex_en: An audition is not a judgment of who you are, but a chance to show who you can become when you dare to try.
ex_zh: The bravest act is not winning the part; it is walking into the room, heart pounding, and saying, “Here I am.”

def_en: A large room or building for public gatherings or performances.
quote_en: The school auditorium is where the whole student body gathers for assemblies—its big red curtains and wooden stage hold memories of countless performances.
quote_zh: A packed auditorium on talent show night buzzes with excitement; every seat is filled with families and friends waiting to cheer.
ex_en: An auditorium, when empty, is a promise—a space waiting to be filled with music, applause, and the courage of those who step into the light.
ex_zh: Some of the most important moments happen not on the stage, but in the seats: a friend’s encouraging smile, a parent’s proud tears.

def_en: Relating to the sense of hearing.
quote_en: If you are an auditory learner, you remember spelling words better when you say them aloud or hear them in a song.
quote_zh: An auditory memory like your grandmother’s lullaby can stay with you your whole life, a soft melody tucked inside your heart.
ex_en: The world speaks to us in sounds—the wind in the trees, the rhythm of footsteps, a friend’s “It’s okay.” Listen, and you will hear wisdom.
ex_zh: A quiet moment is not silent; it is full of the subtle auditory world we so often rush past.

def_en: A mass of snow, ice, and rocks falling rapidly down a mountainside.
def_zh: avalanche of snow; avalanche of homework
quote_en: An avalanche of snow can roar down a mountain, but skiers and rangers study the slopes to keep everyone safe.
quote_zh: The week before winter break felt like an avalanche of homework, but finishing it together with friends made it feel like a team challenge instead of a storm.
ex_en: Sometimes our worries gather like an avalanche, but a small act of kindness can be the tree that stops the slide.
ex_zh: Even the biggest avalanche begins with a single flake; small choices, good or bad, build into forces that shape our lives.

def_en: A narrative poem or song; a slow sentimental song.
quote_en: At campfire nights, the counselors sometimes sing a ballad about a hero who faced a dragon—the song makes you feel like you were there.
quote_zh: An old ballad passed down through generations tells of a village that saved itself by working together, a lesson that never grows old.
ex_en: A ballad is a story that refuses to be forgotten; it travels from heart to heart, carrying hope like a torch in the dark.
ex_zh: Sing your own ballad, not with a perfect voice, but with a life that someone will want to remember.

def_en: Heavy material used to provide stability; something that steadies.
def_zh: ballast for a ship; emotional ballast
quote_en: A ship needs ballast—heavy rocks or water in its hull—to stay upright in rough seas; without it, even a strong wind can tip it over.
quote_zh: Good friends can be your emotional ballast; when you feel tossed about by worry, they steady you with a simple “I’ve got your back.”
ex_en: Integrity is the ballast of character: invisible in calm waters, but essential when storms rise.
ex_zh: Find what steadies you—a song, a friend, a quiet habit—and you will weather any wave.

def_zh: ban a game; ban unfair rules
quote_en: Some schools ban a game if it becomes unsafe, but they usually replace it with something just as fun.
quote_zh: You can ban unfair rules in your own club by speaking up respectfully and suggesting a better way that includes everyone.
ex_en: Banning a book does not erase its ideas; it only makes curious minds search harder.
ex_zh: The strongest force is not what we ban, but what we choose to build: bridges of understanding that make walls unnecessary.

def_en: Declared legally unable to pay debts; exhausted of resources.
quote_en: A company can go bankrupt if it spends more money than it earns, but people who lose their jobs can start over with creativity and help from neighbors.
quote_zh: A person who lies and cheats may seem clever at first, but eventually they become morally bankrupt—empty of the trust that holds friendships together.
ex_en: Bankruptcy is not the end; many who have lost everything have built again, because a fresh start is always possible.
ex_zh: It is better to be poor in pocket than bankrupt in kindness; a generous spirit can rebuild what gold cannot.

def_zh: build a barricade; barricade against bullies
quote_en: During a snow day, kids build a barricade of snowballs to protect their fort—a game that turns a driveway into a castle.
quote_zh: A good school community is the best barricade against bullies; when kids stand together, cruelty finds no foothold.
ex_en: The barricades we build to keep others out often become the walls that keep ourselves in.
ex_zh: True safety is not a barricade, but a circle of trusted friends who have promised to lift each other up.

def_en: Stuck without wind to move a ship; motionless; stalled.
quote_en: If your sailboat becomes becalmed in the middle of the lake, you have to paddle or wait for the wind to return—a chance to notice the quiet ripples.
quote_zh: A ship becalmed on a glassy sea might feel stuck, but sailors used the stillness to rest, tell stories, and watch dolphins play.
ex_en: Being becalmed isn’t failure; sometimes the pause lets you see where you really want to go.
ex_zh: When life feels becalmed, remember: no wind lasts forever, and stillness often brings the clearest view.

def_en: To become friends with; to act kindly toward.
def_zh: befriend a new student; befriend someone lonely
quote_en: The easiest way to befriend a new student is to invite them to sit with you at lunch and ask about their old school.
quote_zh: You can befriend someone lonely by noticing when they’re standing alone at recess and asking, “Want to play?”
ex_en: To befriend another is to say, “I see you, and you matter.”
ex_zh: The friends you befriend when no one else does are the ones who stay beside you longest.

def_zh: befuddle with riddles; befuddle the mind
quote_en: My little brother tried to befuddle me with riddles, but I guessed “a shadow” before he could even finish.
quote_zh: Too many video game rules at once can befuddle the mind—it’s better to learn one thing at a time.
ex_en: A clever trick may befuddle for a moment, but honesty never confuses the heart.
ex_zh: Let wisdom, not confusion, befuddle your doubts—and clarity will find you.

def_en: To express sorrow or distress; to lament.
def_zh: bemoan a loss; bemoan bad luck
quote_en: It’s okay to bemoan a loss when your favorite team loses the championship, but next season you’ll cheer them on again.
quote_zh: Instead of bemoaning bad luck when it rains on picnic day, we brought the games inside and made it even more fun.
ex_en: To bemoan what’s gone is natural; to grow from it is wise.
ex_zh: A life spent bemoaning closed doors misses the windows that have opened.

def_en: Swollen with fluid or excess; puffed up; excessively large.
quote_en: Eating too much candy at once can leave you with a bloated stomach and a reminder that a little treat goes a long way.
quote_zh: After winning the spelling bee, he became bloated with pride and stopped practicing—until he lost the next one.
ex_en: A bloated ego is like a balloon: a small prick of truth and it deflates.
ex_zh: Better to be humble and full of curiosity than bloated and empty of growth.

def_en: A small, thin book with paper covers.
quote_en: The instruction booklet for your new LEGO set shows you step by step how to build a castle with a drawbridge.
quote_zh: An activity booklet on a road trip is filled with mazes, crosswords, and jokes that make the miles fly by.
ex_en: A humble booklet can hold the wisdom that changes a life; don’t judge a book by its thickness.
ex_zh: The most precious booklets are the ones you make yourself—filled with your own drawings, poems, and memories.

quote_en: After a summer of sunshine and rain, the garden produced a bounteous harvest of tomatoes, cucumbers, and pumpkins.
quote_zh: The new student was greeted with bounteous kindness—everyone offered to share their snacks and show her around.
ex_en: A bounteous heart does not count what it gives; it counts the joy that giving brings.
ex_zh: The world’s most bounteous gifts are not gold, but the hands that help and the voices that say, “You belong.”

def_en: To bend forward; to submit or yield; to incline.
def_zh: bow to the king; bow with respect
quote_en: In some cultures, people bow to the king or queen as a sign of honor, like bending a branch in the wind.
quote_zh: At the end of the play, the actors bow with respect to the audience, who clap to thank them for their hard work.
ex_en: To bow before truth is not weakness; it is the strength to let go of what you thought you knew.
ex_zh: The tallest trees bow in the storm; the ones that don’t snap.

def_en: A device for slowing or stopping motion.
quote_en: When you ride a bike, you apply the brake gently before a turn so you don’t skid on loose gravel.
quote_zh: On a roller coaster, the emergency brake is there just in case—but you hope you’ll never need it.
ex_en: Sometimes the wisest action is to apply the brake before you’ve gone too far.
ex_zh: A pause is not a stop; it’s the brake that lets you steer more clearly when you go again.

def_en: To separate into pieces; to fracture; to interrupt.
def_zh: break a rule; break a record
quote_en: If you accidentally break a rule, the bravest thing is to admit it and try to make things right.
quote_zh: The track star trained for months to break the school record, and when she did, the whole team cheered.
ex_en: Some things must break to become whole—a seed breaks open to become a tree, a heart breaks to learn compassion.
ex_zh: You can break a chain of unkindness with a single act of courage.

def_en: A rich fabric woven with raised patterns.
quote_en: In old fairy tales, kings wore brocade fabric with gold threads that shimmered in the candlelight.
quote_zh: For the school play, the costume designer sewed a brocade robe for the wizard, making him look truly magical.
ex_en: Brocade may dress the body, but character clothes the soul.
ex_zh: The finest brocade cannot hide a hollow heart, nor can simple cloth hide a generous one.

quote_en: At the rodeo, a cowboy tries to ride a bronco for eight seconds while the horse bucks and twists in the air.
quote_zh: The bronco bucking in the corral reminded everyone that wild things can’t be forced—they must be won with patience.
ex_en: A bronco’s spirit is not meant for breaking; it’s meant for understanding.
ex_zh: There’s a little bronco in all of us: wild, free, waiting for the right hand to guide, not to tame.

def_en: Energetic and noisy activity; a lively commotion.
quote_en: The morning bustle in our house—toothbrushes, backpacks, and calls for lost shoes—is chaos, but it’s our chaos.
quote_zh: The city bustle of cars and crowds can be exciting, but sometimes it’s nice to find a quiet park bench and just watch.
ex_en: In the bustle of daily life, don’t forget to pause and notice the people around you.
ex_zh: A life of only bustle is a song without rests; the silence between notes makes the music meaningful.

def_en: A small shelter or hut, often near a beach or pool.
quote_en: We rented a pool cabana for the day, a shady spot where we could leave our towels and eat watermelon without sand.
quote_zh: A beach cabana with a thatched roof feels like your own little island, even if you’re only steps from the waves.
ex_en: A cabana is just a shelter; what makes it home is the laughter of friends inside.
ex_zh: Sometimes the simplest cabana, shared with people you love, is the grandest palace.

def_en: A chemical element essential for bones and teeth.
quote_en: The calcium in milk and yogurt helps your bones grow strong so you can run, jump, and climb without worry.
quote_zh: If you don’t like milk, you can get calcium from leafy greens like kale—your bones don’t care where it comes from.
ex_en: Strong bones need calcium; a strong character needs truth. Both take time and daily care.
ex_zh: The calcium of the soul is kindness: build it daily, and you will stand firm.

def_en: A unit of energy from food.
quote_en: Some athletes count calories to make sure they have enough energy for their sport, but they never skip meals.
quote_zh: Your calorie intake should come from colorful foods—fruits, veggies, grains—not just sugary snacks.
ex_en: A calorie is fuel; what matters is how you use the energy—to help, to create, to grow.
ex_zh: Feed your body well, but feed your spirit with the calorie of wonder: books, art, the great outdoors.

def_en: An artificial waterway for navigation or irrigation.
quote_en: A canal boat tour through Amsterdam lets you see the city from the water, gliding under old stone bridges.
quote_zh: The Panama Canal is a famous waterway that lets ships cross from the Atlantic to the Pacific without going around South America.
ex_en: A canal is a channel carved by patience; it turns dry land into a path for trade and life.
ex_zh: When obstacles block your way, sometimes you build a canal—a new route that water and dreams can follow.

def_en: The ability to hold, receive, or contain; potential.
quote_en: The carrying capacity of a backpack is how much it can hold before the zipper pops—we tested that on camping trips.
quote_zh: Every brain has a nearly endless capacity for learning; you just have to keep asking questions and stay curious.
ex_en: The capacity to forgive is a muscle; the more you use it, the stronger it grows.
ex_zh: You have a far greater capacity for kindness than you know; stretch it every day.

def_en: A point of land extending into water; a sleeveless garment.
def_zh: Cape of Good Hope; superhero cape
quote_en: The Cape of Good Hope in South Africa is where sailors used to think the world ended, but it was just a new beginning.
quote_zh: A superhero cape might be just an old towel tied around your neck, but when you wear it, you feel like you can do anything.
ex_en: A cape of land points toward the unknown; courage is standing at its edge.
ex_zh: The best capes are invisible: the kindness you wear every day.

def_en: To overturn in water; to upset.
quote_en: We practiced what to do if the canoe capsizes—stay calm, hold onto the boat, and swim to shore together.
quote_zh: A big wave can capsize a small boat, but sailors learn to read the water and avoid dangerous conditions.
ex_en: Plans can capsize, but a steady heart can right itself again.
ex_zh: What capsizes you today will teach you how to stay afloat tomorrow.

def_en: Goods carried by a ship, aircraft, or vehicle.
quote_en: A cargo ship carries containers across the ocean—everything from bananas to bicycles arrives by sea.
quote_zh: The charity sent a cargo of toys to children who had lost everything in the storm, a gift that brought smiles back.
ex_en: The cargo we carry in our hearts—gratitude, kindness, hope—is the most valuable freight.
ex_zh: Travel light in things, but heavy in love.

def_en: A dish baked in a deep container; the container itself.
quote_en: When our neighbor was sick, our family brought a chicken casserole—it was a small thing, but it said “we care.”
quote_zh: The potluck at school had everything from lasagna to a veggie casserole that even the picky eaters tried.
ex_en: A casserole is more than food; it’s warmth in a dish, a way of saying “you’re not alone.”
ex_zh: The best recipes are the ones you share; the best meals are the ones you eat together.

def_en: A device for launching projectiles; v. to launch suddenly.
quote_en: In history class, we built a small medieval catapult with popsicle sticks and launched marshmallows across the room.
quote_zh: A movie role can catapult an unknown actor into fame overnight, but real success takes more than luck.
ex_en: Small efforts, consistently made, can catapult you farther than any single leap.
ex_zh: A catapult needs tension to launch; sometimes the struggles we face are the tension that propels us forward.

def_en: Relating to the sky or heavens; divine.
quote_en: A celestial body like the moon or Jupiter can be seen with a simple telescope on a clear night.
quote_zh: A celestial event such as a meteor shower is a perfect excuse to stay up late, lie on a blanket, and make wishes.
ex_en: The celestial map has guided sailors for centuries; let your values be your stars.
ex_zh: We are all made of stardust—celestial beings learning to be kind on a small blue planet.

def_en: A fatty substance in the blood; associated with heart disease.
quote_en: Your doctor checks cholesterol levels to make sure your heart stays healthy—eating oatmeal and veggies helps.
quote_zh: Foods like fried chicken can raise high cholesterol, so it’s smart to balance treats with fruits and exercise.
ex_en: High cholesterol clogs arteries; a clogged heart can’t pump love freely.
ex_zh: Feed your heart with movement, laughter, and the people who fill it with joy.

def_en: To move or flow continuously through a system.
quote_en: Opening two windows lets air circulate through the house, carrying away the heat on a summer afternoon.
quote_zh: Students circulated a petition to get a new water fountain near the playground—and it worked.
ex_en: Kindness circulates like oxygen: breathe it in, breathe it out, and the whole room comes alive.
ex_zh: What you circulate—gossip or hope—grows. Choose what you want to multiply.

def_en: The distance around a circle or sphere; the outer boundary.
def_zh: measure the circumference; circumference of a tree
quote_en: In math class, we used a string to measure the circumference of a tree trunk, then divided by pi to find its age.
quote_zh: The circumference of a pizza is the distance around the edge—and the part everyone fights over for the crunchy crust.
ex_en: The circumference of your concern should widen with each year, embracing more of the world.
ex_zh: You can’t measure the circumference of a heart; it expands every time you care.

def_en: An advanced stage of human social development.
quote_en: The ancient civilization of the Maya built incredible pyramids and knew more about the stars than we once thought.
quote_zh: When a civilization collapses, it’s often because people stopped cooperating—a lesson we should never forget.
ex_en: A civilization is not its monuments, but how it treats its weakest members.
ex_zh: Build your own civilization: a family, a team, a community where everyone belongs.

def_en: A group of families with a common ancestor; a close-knit group.
quote_en: A Scottish clan would gather under their tartan banner for festivals, sharing music and stories for generations.
quote_zh: My soccer team became a clan of friends; we won and lost together, and that bond lasted long after the final game.
ex_en: A clan is not blood alone; it is anyone who has your back when you need it most.
ex_zh: Find your clan—the people who make you brave enough to be yourself.

def_en: The prevailing weather conditions; the general atmosphere.
quote_en: Scientists study climate change to understand how our planet is warming and how we can protect it for future generations.
quote_zh: The school climate felt happier when everyone started using the buddy bench at recess.
ex_en: The climate of your heart is set by the weather of your thoughts.
ex_zh: You can change the climate of a room with a single smile.

def_en: Rough; crude; not fine or delicate.
quote_en: Coarse sandpaper is used to smooth rough wood, but you have to start rough to get to smooth.
quote_zh: Using coarse language can hurt feelings; choosing kind words keeps friendships strong.
ex_en: A coarse exterior may hide a gentle heart; don’t judge by the surface.
ex_zh: Life’s rough patches are coarse sandpaper—they smooth you if you let them.

def_en: An artwork made by assembling various materials.
quote_en: For art class, we made a collage using magazine cutouts, fabric scraps, and even dried leaves from the park.
quote_zh: A photo collage of summer camp—swimming, hiking, laughing—captures memories that one picture couldn’t hold.
ex_en: Life is a collage: pieces that seem mismatched become beautiful when you see the whole picture.
ex_zh: Every friendship is a scrap in the collage of your life; together they make a masterpiece.

def_zh: make a commitment; commitment to practice
quote_en: When you make a commitment to join the soccer team, you show up for practice even when you’re tired.
quote_zh: Her commitment to practice every day turned her from a shaky beginner into a confident musician.
ex_en: Commitment isn’t about never failing; it’s about getting back up when you do.
ex_zh: The deepest commitments are the ones you keep to yourself: to be kind, to be brave, to try again.

def_en: A state of confused and noisy disturbance.
def_zh: cause a commotion; commotion in the hallway
quote_en: When the fire drill sounded, the commotion in the hallway was loud but orderly—everyone knew where to go.
quote_zh: A squirrel in the cafeteria caused a commotion, scrambling over trays while kids laughed and pointed.
ex_en: In the commotion of life, find the calm center within you.
ex_zh: Not every commotion needs your reaction; sometimes stillness speaks louder.

quote_en: It took me a while to comprehend the concept of fractions, but once I did, math became a puzzle I could solve.
quote_zh: It’s hard to fully comprehend how much your parents love you—until you have someone to care for yourself.
ex_en: To comprehend another’s pain, you don’t need to have lived it; you only need to listen.
ex_zh: The mind comprehends facts; the heart comprehends what matters.

def_en: To create by mixing ingredients; to devise or invent.
def_zh: concoct a story; concoct a potion
quote_en: For Halloween, we concocted a “witches’ brew” from apple juice, green food coloring, and gummy worms.
quote_zh: My little brother can concoct a story from anything—a lost shoe becomes a dragon’s treasure in his telling.
ex_en: To concoct a lie is to build on sand; to concoct a kindness is to plant a tree.
ex_zh: The best concoctions are the ones made with care and shared with joy.

quote_en: In geometry, congruent shapes are the same size and shape, even if one is rotated or flipped.
quote_zh: A team works best when their goals are congruent—everyone pulling the same direction.
ex_en: Let your actions be congruent with your values; then you will never have to explain yourself.
ex_zh: When your heart and your head are congruent, you move with the grace of a river.

def_en: Large in size, amount, or importance; noteworthy.
quote_en: It took considerable effort to build the school garden, but now we have fresh vegetables for lunch.
quote_zh: A little kindness can make a considerable difference in someone’s day—a smile, a “good job,” a seat saved.
ex_en: Considerable wealth may impress; considerable character endures.
ex_zh: The most considerable gift you can give is your full attention.

def_en: Unchanging; reliable; acting in the same way over time.
quote_en: Consistent effort in spelling practice turned my C into an A—not magic, just sticking with it.
quote_zh: The best piano players make consistent practice a habit, even when they’d rather be playing outside.
ex_en: A consistent friend is a rare treasure: reliable as sunrise, steady as a heartbeat.
ex_zh: Great things are not done by sudden leaps but by consistent, quiet steps.

def_en: To eat or use up; to absorb entirely.
quote_en: Running around the playground can consume a lot of energy, which is why you need a healthy snack afterward.
quote_zh: In the school cafeteria, we consumed our meal quickly so we’d have time to trade Pokémon cards.
ex_en: What you consume—media, food, ideas—shapes who you become. Choose wisely.
ex_zh: Don’t let worry consume you; let hope consume you instead.

def_en: The act of using up resources; the amount consumed.
quote_en: Turning off lights when you leave a room helps reduce consumption of electricity, which is good for the planet.
quote_zh: The consumption of sugary drinks at the school fair was so high they ran out of cups before lunch.
ex_en: Mindful consumption honors the hands that made what you use.
ex_zh: The earth’s resources are not endless; consumption without gratitude steals from tomorrow.

def_en: Heated disagreement; a point argued in a debate.
def_zh: point of contention; bone of contention
quote_en: The point of contention between the two teams was whether the ball was in or out—the referee had the final say.
quote_zh: Who got to choose the movie was a bone of contention until we agreed to take turns.
ex_en: In every contention, seek understanding before victory.
ex_zh: The strongest argument is not the loudest, but the one that builds a bridge.

quote_en: The seven continents are Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Europe, North America, Australia, and South America.
quote_zh: The continent of Africa is home to the Sahara Desert, the world’s largest hot desert, and the longest river, the Nile.
ex_en: Every continent has its own stories; listen, and you’ll find we are more alike than different.
ex_zh: We are citizens of a continent of compassion, connected by our shared humanity.

def_en: Opposite; opposed in nature or direction.
quote_en: Contrary to expectations, the underdog team won the championship, proving that hard work beats luck.
quote_zh: The sailors faced contrary winds that pushed them off course, but they adjusted their sails and kept going.
ex_en: When life seems contrary, don’t fight the wind—adjust your sails.
ex_zh: The contrary voice may be the one that keeps you honest; listen for truth, not comfort.

def_en: To persuade by argument or evidence.
def_zh: convince your parents; convince a friend
quote_en: You might convince your parents to let you have a pet by showing them you can be responsible with chores.
quote_zh: I couldn’t convince my friend that the movie would be good, so we compromised and watched a comedy instead.
ex_en: To convince others, first listen; a closed ear convinces no one.
ex_zh: The best arguments don’t shout; they whisper, “I understand.”

def_en: To gather and confine; to enclose.
def_zh: corral the horses; corral the kids
quote_en: At the ranch, we had to corral the horses before the storm came, calling them in with buckets of oats.
quote_zh: Trying to corral the kids for a group photo felt like herding cats—everyone ran in different directions.
ex_en: Don’t corral your dreams in a pen of fear; let them run wild.
ex_zh: A heart that corrals kindness is a heart that grows.

def_en: To communicate by writing; to match or agree.
def_zh: correspond by email; correspond with a pen pal
quote_en: For years, my grandma and her best friend corresponded by email, sharing photos and memories across the country.
quote_zh: My map skills improved when I had to correspond with a pen pal from Japan, learning about her town and school.
ex_en: Letters correspond across time; a written word can touch a heart long after it’s read.
ex_zh: Let your actions correspond with your words, and you will be trusted.

quote_en: A corrupt official might take money meant for schools, leaving classrooms without books—a betrayal of trust.
quote_zh: In some stories, a corrupt system makes the rich richer while the poor struggle, until brave people stand up to change it.
ex_en: A corrupt heart cannot see virtue; it sees only what it has lost.
ex_zh: The antidote to corruption is not wealth, but courage to speak what is true.

def_en: A group convened for advice or governance.
quote_en: The student council organized a bake sale to raise money for new playground equipment.
quote_zh: The city council meets every Tuesday to discuss parks, roads, and how to keep the community safe and happy.
ex_en: A wise council listens more than it speaks.
ex_zh: You don’t need a title to serve; every classroom has its own council of kind hearts.

quote_en: Before making a big decision, it’s smart to seek counsel from parents or teachers who have more experience.
quote_zh: The king relied on the wise counsel of his advisors, who reminded him that a leader serves, not rules.
ex_en: Counsel freely given is a gift; counsel freely received is wisdom.
ex_zh: The best counsel often comes from those who have walked the path before you.

def_en: Two lines of verse, usually rhyming.
quote_en: For poetry class, we had to write a couplet about spring: “The sun is warm, the birds all sing / And joy is such a simple thing.”
quote_zh: Shakespeare often ended scenes with a rhyming couplet, giving the audience a moment to think and applaud.
ex_en: A couplet is small, but it can hold the whole sky of feeling.
ex_zh: In two lines, you can say something that lasts a lifetime.

def_en: The path or direction taken; a series of lessons.
quote_en: When the trail was blocked by a fallen tree, we had to change course and find another way home.
quote_zh: The main course at dinner was lasagna, but the best part was the garlic bread.
ex_en: Life’s course is not a straight line; the detours often hold the best views.
ex_zh: You can always change your course; a new direction is a new beginning.

def_en: A skilled worker who makes things by hand.
quote_en: A master craftsman can turn a simple piece of wood into a chair that lasts for generations.
quote_zh: The craftsman’s skill was passed down from grandparent to grandchild, each adding their own touch.
ex_en: A craftsman knows that beauty takes time; rushing ruins the work.
ex_zh: Be a craftsman of your own life: shape it with care, patience, and love.

def_en: To desire intensely; to yearn for.
quote_en: Some people crave attention so much they act out, but real connection comes from being yourself.
quote_zh: After dinner, I always crave something sweet, but sometimes an apple is just as satisfying as cake.
ex_en: What you crave the most may be what you need the least; listen to your wiser self.
ex_zh: Crave understanding more than approval, and you’ll find both.

quote_en: It’s tempting to take credit for a group project, but sharing credit builds stronger friendships.
quote_zh: In the presentation, we made sure to give credit to the websites and books that helped us with our research.
ex_en: Credit is not a pie—giving some away doesn’t leave you with less.
ex_zh: The credit you give others comes back to you as trust.

def_en: The scientific study of crime and criminals.
quote_en: Some people study criminology to understand why crimes happen and how to prevent them, making communities safer.
quote_zh: The principles of criminology show that poverty and lack of opportunity can lead people to crime—so the best solution is to help everyone thrive.
ex_en: Criminology teaches that justice is not revenge; it is healing the harm.
ex_zh: A safer world begins with understanding, not punishment.

def_en: The customs, arts, and achievements of a society.
quote_en: Learning about different cultures through food, music, and stories helps us appreciate how beautiful our world is.
quote_zh: The school culture changed when teachers started greeting every student by name at the door—everyone felt seen.
ex_en: Culture is not a museum; it is a living river, always flowing, always becoming.
ex_zh: Build a culture of kindness in your corner of the world, and watch it spread.

def_en: Awkward; difficult to carry or manage.
quote_en: A cumbersome backpack stuffed with textbooks can make your shoulders ache—pack only what you need for the day.
quote_zh: The old way of checking out library books was a cumbersome process with cards and stamps, but now it’s fast and easy.
ex_en: Some rules become cumbersome when they forget why they were made; simplify what no longer serves.
ex_zh: Let go of what is cumbersome—not just in your bag, but in your heart.

def_en: A regulation requiring people to be indoors at a specified time.
quote_en: My parents set a curfew of 8:30 on school nights so I can rest and be ready for tomorrow.
quote_zh: If you break curfew, you might lose privileges, but explaining why you were late shows responsibility.
ex_en: A curfew isn’t meant to box you in; it’s a fence of love to keep you safe.
ex_zh: When you respect curfew, you’re building trust—a key that unlocks more freedom.

def_en: Money in circulation; acceptance or prevalence.
quote_en: Before our trip to Canada, we exchanged dollars for foreign currency—the bills looked different but spent the same.
quote_zh: Digital currency like Bitcoin is used online, but you can’t hold it in your hand like a coin.
ex_en: The truest currency is trust; it buys what money cannot.
ex_zh: Invest your time in relationships; their value never inflates.

def_en: Having the shape of a cylinder.
quote_en: A cylindrical can of soup fits neatly in the pantry, and the label tells you what’s inside.
quote_zh: The cylindrical tower of the castle was once used to watch for enemies approaching across the sea.
ex_en: A cylindrical shape has no beginning or end; like kindness, it circles back.
ex_zh: Be cylindrical in spirit: stand tall, roll with life, and hold what matters inside.

def_en: Seeming difficult to deal with; intimidating.
quote_en: Cleaning out the garage felt like a daunting task, but with everyone helping, we finished in an afternoon.
quote_zh: Climbing the rope in gym class was a daunting challenge until a friend showed me how to use my legs.
ex_en: What seems daunting at first often becomes a story you tell with pride.
ex_zh: Every mountain is daunting until you take the first step.

def_en: To waste time; to move slowly.
def_zh: dawdle on the way; dawdle over homework
quote_en: If you dawdle on the way to school, you might miss the bell—and the morning announcements.
quote_zh: It’s okay to dawdle over homework when you’re truly stuck, but a timer can help you focus.
ex_en: To dawdle is human; to choose your pace with purpose is wisdom.
ex_zh: Sometimes dawdling is not laziness but the soul catching up.

quote_en: After the storm, volunteers helped clear debris from the sidewalks so people could walk safely.
quote_zh: Scientists track space debris—old satellites and rocket parts—to prevent collisions with working spacecraft.
ex_en: From debris, life can grow; a forest rises from ash, a garden from rubble.
ex_zh: Clear the debris of old grudges to make room for new friendship.

def_en: To cause to believe what is false; to mislead.
quote_en: It’s easy to deceive yourself into thinking you studied enough, but the test will tell the truth.
quote_zh: Cheaters try to deceive others by copying answers, but they only cheat themselves out of learning.
ex_en: To deceive is to build a house on sand; the first wave will wash it away.
ex_zh: Honesty is not just telling the truth; it’s refusing to deceive yourself.

def_en: The act of deceiving; a trick or fraud.
quote_en: The villain in the story wove a web of deception so clever that even the hero almost believed the lies.
quote_zh: Self-deception is pretending you didn’t eat the last cookie when the crumbs are on your shirt.
ex_en: Deception may win a moment, but truth wins a lifetime.
ex_zh: The hardest deception to escape is the one you tell yourself.

def_en: To interpret or decode; to make out the meaning.
quote_en: In spy club, we tried to decipher a code using a secret key that shifted each letter by three.
quote_zh: It took me a while to decipher my grandmother’s handwriting, but her recipes were worth the effort.
ex_en: Some truths can’t be spoken; they must be deciphered with patience and an open heart.
ex_zh: Decipher the silence of a friend; sometimes the loudest cries are wordless.

quote_en: A vitamin deficiency can make you tired, which is why eating colorful fruits and veggies is so important.
quote_zh: I had a deficiency in math skills, but extra practice with my dad turned it into a strength.
ex_en: Acknowledging a deficiency is the first step toward growth.
ex_zh: You are not defined by what you lack, but by what you do with what you have.

def_en: A state of low spirits; sadness; despair.
def_zh: feeling of dejection; sink into dejection
quote_en: After losing the championship, a feeling of dejection swept over the team until the coach reminded them how far they’d come.
quote_zh: It’s easy to sink into dejection when things go wrong, but talking to a friend can lift the clouds.
ex_en: Dejection is winter in the soul; spring always follows.
ex_zh: Even in dejection, a spark of hope remains—fan it gently.

def_en: Reliable; trustworthy; able to be depended upon.
quote_en: A dependable friend shows up when they say they will, even if it means missing a fun game.
quote_zh: Our old station wagon was a dependable car—it never broke down, even on long road trips.
ex_en: Dependability is a quiet superpower; it builds trust without a single word.
ex_zh: Be the dependable one in someone’s life; they will remember your steadiness forever.

def_en: Relying on someone or something for support.
def_zh: dependent on parents; dependent on technology
quote_en: Young children are dependent on parents for food, shelter, and help tying their shoes.
quote_zh: Being too dependent on technology can make you forget how to figure things out on your own.
ex_en: Independence grows when you learn to lean on yourself, but healthy dependence on others is part of being human.
ex_zh: To be dependent is not weakness; we all stand on shoulders of those who came before.

def_en: Severe despondency; economic downturn; a low area.
quote_en: It’s normal to feel depression after a loss, but talking to someone you trust can help you find your way back.
quote_zh: The Great Depression was a time when many people lost jobs and homes, but communities came together to help each other.
ex_en: Depression is a fog, not a destination; the sun still shines behind it.
ex_zh: When depression whispers you are alone, remember the hands ready to hold you.

quote_en: A derisive laugh from the back of the room made the presenter stumble, but she took a breath and continued.
quote_zh: Derisive comments about someone’s clothes can wound more deeply than a punch.
ex_en: Mockery is the weapon of the insecure; kindness is the shield of the strong.
ex_zh: Turn a derisive word into a chance to show grace.

def_en: To plan or invent; to contrive.
def_zh: devise a plan; devise a solution
quote_en: We had to devise a plan to raise money for the animal shelter—a bake sale, a car wash, and a talent show.
quote_zh: The engineers worked together to devise a solution for cleaning plastic from the ocean.
ex_en: To devise a dream is the first step; to live it is the rest.
ex_zh: The best plans are devised not alone, but with the wisdom of many.

quote_en: A diligent student doesn’t just study hard; they ask questions, take notes, and help others understand.
quote_zh: With diligent effort, I finally learned to play “Ode to Joy” on the piano—every wrong note was a step closer.
ex_en: Diligence is the bridge between wanting and having.
ex_zh: The diligent hand shapes the world; the patient heart sustains it.

def_en: To release; to dismiss; to emit.
def_zh: discharge a patient; discharge a duty
quote_en: The hospital will discharge a patient when they are well enough to go home and rest.
quote_zh: After the project was done, we discharged our duty by cleaning up the workspace together.
ex_en: To discharge a kindness is to let it flow through you to others.
ex_zh: Some duties are a burden; others, when discharged with love, become a gift.

def_en: To reject; to send away; to disregard.
def_zh: dismiss an idea; dismiss a class
quote_en: The teacher didn’t dismiss my idea just because it was unusual; she asked me to explain it further.
quote_zh: At the end of the day, the principal dismisses the class over the loudspeaker, and everyone heads home.
ex_en: Don’t dismiss a person because they are different; difference is the seed of discovery.
ex_zh: What you dismiss today may be the answer you need tomorrow.

quote_en: The two friends had a dispute over whose turn it was to choose the game, so they decided to take turns.
quote_zh: The ownership of the old treehouse was in dispute until we agreed it belonged to everyone.
ex_en: In every dispute, there is a chance to learn, if both sides are willing to listen.
ex_zh: A dispute resolved with respect leaves both sides stronger.

def_en: To interrupt; to throw into disorder.
quote_en: Shouting across the room can disrupt the class and make it harder for everyone to learn.
quote_zh: A loud thunderstorm might disrupt your sleep, but the morning after, the air feels fresh.
ex_en: Disruption can be the beginning of change; from chaos, new patterns emerge.
ex_zh: Don’t let a little disruption knock you off course; adjust and keep going.

quote_en: Most people write with their dominant hand, but some are ambidextrous and can use both.
quote_zh: In the forest, wolves are a dominant species, not by cruelty but by working together.
ex_en: A dominant voice can lead, but a listening heart makes the leader.
ex_zh: The strongest person is not the one who dominates, but the one who lifts others up.

def_en: A succession of rulers from the same family.
quote_en: The Ming dynasty in China is famous for its art, porcelain, and the Forbidden City.
quote_zh: The Chicago Bulls were a basketball dynasty in the 1990s, winning six championships in eight years.
ex_en: A dynasty may crumble, but the culture it built lives on in stories.
ex_zh: You are not bound by your family’s past; you can start a new dynasty of kindness.

def_en: Sparing in use of resources; thrifty.
quote_en: An economical car uses less gas, which is better for the environment and your wallet.
quote_zh: My grandmother was economical with words, but when she spoke, everyone listened.
ex_en: Being economical with resources is wisdom; being economical with kindness is a loss.
ex_zh: The most economical life is not the cheapest, but the one that wastes nothing of value.

def_zh: eject a disk; eject a player
quote_en: The DVD player will eject the disk when you press the button, ready for the next movie.
quote_zh: In a game, a referee might eject a player who breaks the rules, but they can learn from the mistake.
ex_en: Life may eject you from a comfortable place, but that’s how you find new ground.
ex_zh: Don’t eject hope when it seems faint; it’s the spark that lights the way.

def_en: To issue or flow out from a source.
quote_en: A campfire emanates warmth that draws everyone to sit around and share stories.
quote_zh: A good leader can emanate confidence, making the team feel they can accomplish anything.
ex_en: Kindness emanates from the heart; it cannot be faked.
ex_zh: Let your light emanate so brightly that others feel braver just being near you.

def_en: The sovereign ruler of an empire.
quote_en: The Roman emperor Augustus built roads and aqueducts that still stand two thousand years later.
quote_zh: The emperor penguin endures the coldest winter on Earth to protect its egg—a true ruler of resilience.
ex_en: An emperor may command armies, but a child’s laughter commands a different kind of power.
ex_zh: True authority is not given by a crown, but earned by care.

def_zh: encounter a bear; encounter a problem
quote_en: While hiking, we hoped to see deer but didn’t expect to encounter a bear—it was terrifying and thrilling.
quote_zh: In coding, you might encounter a problem that makes no sense, but asking for help can solve it fast.
ex_en: Every encounter is a chance to learn something new, if you keep your eyes open.
ex_zh: The best encounters are with people who see the real you.

def_zh: encroach on land; encroach on privacy
quote_en: Developers wanted to build houses that would encroach on the nature preserve, but the community protected it.
quote_zh: When a sibling reads your diary, they encroach on your privacy; a conversation about boundaries helps.
ex_en: Progress should not encroach on what is precious; build without destroying.
ex_zh: Respect the space of others, and they will respect yours.

quote_en: Trying to endear yourself by bragging usually backfires; being genuine works better.
quote_zh: The puppy’s clumsy tumble endear her to everyone in the family—she’s impossible not to love.
ex_en: What endears is not perfection, but the courage to be imperfectly yourself.
ex_zh: Small kindnesses endear you more than grand gestures.

quote_en: An energetic puppy needs lots of walks and playtime to burn off all that excitement.
quote_zh: The dance team’s energetic performance had the whole crowd clapping and cheering.
ex_en: Energy is a gift; use it to build, to create, to uplift.
ex_zh: An energetic heart can tire, but a heart fueled by purpose never runs out.

def_zh: engulf in flames; engulf in work
quote_en: The old barn was engulfed in flames, but the firefighters saved the animals inside.
quote_zh: Sometimes I feel engulfed in work, but a short break helps me breathe and refocus.
ex_en: Worry can engulf you if you let it, but hope is a life raft.
ex_zh: When you feel engulfed, reach out—someone will pull you back.

quote_en: The enticing smell of fresh cookies drifted from the kitchen, making it hard to wait for dinner.
quote_zh: The store’s enticing offer promised a free toy, but it came with hidden costs.
ex_en: An enticing path may lead nowhere; choose the path that leads to growth.
ex_zh: What is enticing is not always what is good; learn to tell the difference.

quote_en: The United Nations sent a special envoy to help the two countries talk instead of fight.
quote_zh: In the school play, I played a peace envoy who delivered a message that stopped a war.
ex_en: An envoy speaks for another, but their true voice is their own character.
ex_zh: Be an envoy of kindness—carry its message wherever you go.

quote_en: The volcanic eruption in Pompeii buried the city in ash, but preserved it for centuries.
quote_zh: A sudden eruption of laughter came from the back of the class when the hamster escaped its cage.
ex_en: Sometimes an eruption clears the way for new growth; the ash becomes fertile soil.
ex_zh: Emotions need safe release; an eruption avoided is a pressure that builds.

quote_en: Our summer escapade involved kayaking to a tiny island, where we pretended we were explorers.
quote_zh: The twins’ mischievous escapade—switching places in class—fooled the substitute for almost an hour.
ex_en: An escapade is a memory waiting to be made; make it a safe one.
ex_zh: Life’s best stories are born from an escapade with friends who laugh with you.

quote_en: We hold our teachers in high esteem because they work so hard to help us learn.
quote_zh: Building self-esteem comes from trying new things, failing, and getting back up.
ex_en: Esteem earned is a treasure; esteem demanded is a counterfeit.
ex_zh: Give esteem freely; you will never run out.

quote_en: It was evident that the rain had stopped when we saw the sun peek through the clouds.
quote_zh: The joy on the kids’ faces was evident when they opened the new playground.
ex_en: The most evident truths are often the ones we overlook.
ex_zh: Let your kindness be evident, even in small things.

def_en: To call forth; to bring to mind.
quote_en: The smell of pine can evoke memories of winter holidays and trips to the mountains.
quote_zh: A powerful story can evoke emotion that stays with you long after you close the book.
ex_en: Some words evoke the best in us; use them often.
ex_zh: Let your life evoke hope in those who watch.

def_en: A statement that represents something as larger or greater than it is.
quote_en: Saying “I waited a million years” is a wild exaggeration, but it gets the point across.
quote_zh: A harmless exaggeration in a tall tale makes the story more fun, as long as no one believes it’s true.
ex_en: Exaggeration may entertain, but truth builds trust.
ex_zh: The tallest tales are built on the strongest truths.

def_en: A short passage taken from a longer work.
quote_en: The teacher read an excerpt from the novel that made the whole class want to read more.
quote_zh: A famous excerpt from Martin Luther King Jr.’s speech is “I have a dream.”
ex_en: An excerpt is a window; sometimes a single sentence can change your view.
ex_zh: From the smallest excerpt, whole worlds can be imagined.

def_en: To leave out; to keep from entering.
def_zh: exclude from the game; exclude from the group
quote_en: It hurts to be excluded from the game because you’re the new kid; the kind thing is to invite someone in.
quote_zh: Some clubs unfairly exclude people who don’t look like them, but that only makes the club smaller.
ex_en: To exclude is to close a door that could have let in light.
ex_zh: Inclusion costs nothing and makes everything richer.

def_en: Restricted to a particular group; not shared.
quote_en: An exclusive club with a secret handshake might feel special, but it leaves many people out.
quote_zh: The reporter got an exclusive interview with the astronaut, the only one who heard her story.
ex_en: Exclusive things can feel precious, but shared things are treasures.
ex_zh: The best experiences are not exclusive; they are the ones we invite others into.

quote_en: Our school excursion to the science museum was the best field trip ever—we saw a real dinosaur skeleton.
quote_zh: A weekend excursion to the beach with my family means building sandcastles and hunting for shells.
ex_en: Every excursion is a chance to see the world with fresh eyes.
ex_zh: The best excursions are not about distance, but about discovery.

def_en: The act of spending; an amount spent.
quote_en: Running a marathon requires a huge energy expenditure, so athletes carbo-load before the race.
quote_zh: The town’s government expenditure on new parks meant more places for kids to play.
ex_en: Expenditure of time on what matters is never wasted.
ex_zh: The wisest expenditure is on things that grow: kindness, knowledge, community.

quote_en: The heroic exploits of firefighters who run into burning buildings inspire us to be brave.
quote_zh: The library has books about the legendary exploits of King Arthur and his knights.
ex_en: True exploits are not always loud; a quiet act of courage can change a life.
ex_zh: Your own exploits begin with a small step today.

def_en: Outside the regular curriculum or duties.
quote_en: Extracurricular activities like soccer, chess, and drama help you make friends and learn new skills.
quote_zh: Joining extracurricular clubs taught me more about teamwork than any textbook.
ex_en: What you do extracurricularly often reveals your true passions.
ex_zh: The best part of school is what you choose to do after the bell rings.

def_en: To hand over a person to another jurisdiction for trial.
def_zh: extradite a criminal; extradite to another country
quote_en: When a criminal flees to another country, the government can request to extradite them to face justice.
quote_zh: In the spy movie, agents worked to extradite the villain back to the country where he committed the crime.
ex_en: Justice sometimes requires crossing borders; so does compassion.
ex_zh: You don’t need to extradite a wrong; you can choose to forgive and let go.

quote_en: When telling a story, leave out extraneous details so the main point shines through.
quote_zh: Extraneous noise in the classroom makes it hard to concentrate, so we agreed to quiet down.
ex_en: Strip away the extraneous; what remains is what truly matters.
ex_zh: A life cluttered with extraneous things leaves no room for joy.

quote_en: Some people claim to have extrasensory perception, like knowing who’s calling before they pick up the phone.
quote_zh: In stories, extrasensory ability might include telepathy or seeing the future, but in real life, we call it intuition.
ex_en: Your extrasensory power is empathy—feeling what others feel without a word.
ex_zh: Listen to that extrasensory whisper; it’s often your best self speaking.

def_en: Spending more than is necessary; lavish.
quote_en: An extravagant gift like a diamond necklace might be beautiful, but a handmade card can mean even more.
quote_zh: Some celebrities live an extravagant lifestyle with private jets and mansions, but that doesn’t guarantee happiness.
ex_en: Extravagance can impress, but simplicity often sustains.
ex_zh: The most extravagant thing you can give is your time.

def_en: To thrust out; to force out.
quote_en: A 3D printer can extrude plastic in thin layers to build a model from a digital design.
quote_zh: When a volcano erupts, it extrudes lava that cools into new rock.
ex_en: Don’t extrude your anger onto others; find a healthy way to release it.
ex_zh: Let your creativity extrude into the world, one small piece at a time.

def_zh: cry of exultation; moment of exultation
quote_en: A cry of exultation went up from the crowd when the home team scored in the last second.
quote_zh: The moment of exultation after finishing the race—even in last place—was pure happiness.
ex_en: Exultation is sweeter when you share it.
ex_zh: Find joy not only in winning, but in the effort that got you there.

def_en: One side of something many-sided; an aspect.
def_zh: facet of a diamond; every facet
quote_en: A diamond has many facets that catch the light; each one adds to its brilliance.
quote_zh: Learning history means exploring every facet—the art, the science, the stories of ordinary people.
ex_en: Every person has a facet you haven’t seen; be curious.
ex_zh: Life’s beauty lies in its facets; turn it and see something new.

quote_en: Organizations provide famine relief by sending food and clean water to places where crops have failed.
quote_zh: In the famine-stricken region, families walked miles for a single meal.
ex_en: Famine teaches us to share, to waste less, and to be grateful for what we have.
ex_zh: A full plate is a blessing; a sharing hand multiplies it.

def_en: A thread-like strand; dietary roughage; moral strength.
quote_en: Dietary fiber in fruits and vegetables helps your digestion; it’s why we say “eat your greens.”
quote_zh: Heroes show moral fiber when they do the right thing even when no one is watching.
ex_en: Your moral fiber is woven by the choices you make each day.
ex_zh: Strong fiber bends without breaking; so does a strong heart.

def_zh: figment of imagination; figment of a dream
quote_en: The monster under the bed was just a figment of my imagination—a shadow that looked scary in the dark.
quote_zh: The story’s dragon was a figment of the author’s dream, but it felt so real.
ex_en: Fear is often a figment that grows when left unchecked.
ex_zh: Dreams start as figments; with courage, they become real.

quote_en: Learning financial literacy in school helps you understand saving, spending, and planning for the future.
quote_zh: Many colleges offer financial aid to students who need help paying tuition.
ex_en: Financial wealth is one kind of wealth; the wealth of experience is another.
ex_zh: True richness is not financial; it is the richness of relationships.

quote_en: It takes forbearance to stay calm when your little brother keeps knocking over your LEGO tower.
quote_zh: The principal’s act of forbearance gave the student a second chance to make things right.
ex_en: Forbearance is not giving up; it’s choosing to give room for growth.
ex_zh: Practice forbearance with yourself; you are still learning.

quote_en: No one can truly foretell the future, but we can make good guesses based on what we know.
quote_zh: Meteorologists foretell the weather using satellites and radar, but sometimes they get it wrong.
ex_en: You cannot foretell the twists of life, but you can prepare your heart for any path.
ex_zh: What you do today foretells who you will become tomorrow.

quote_en: The principal will formally announce the winner of the essay contest at assembly.
quote_zh: For the wedding, everyone was formally dressed in suits and dresses, even the flower girl.
ex_en: Being formally introduced is nice, but being truly known is better.
ex_zh: Sometimes the most important things are said informally—in whispers, in glances, in shared laughter.

quote_en: The artist formerly known as Prince changed his name to a symbol for a while.
quote_zh: My neighbor was formerly a teacher, and now she volunteers at the school library.
ex_en: What you were formerly does not define what you can become.
ex_zh: The path formerly traveled can lead to new places.

def_zh: bone fracture; fracture in the rock
quote_en: A bone fracture needs time to heal; the doctor said six weeks in a cast.
quote_zh: The climber found a fracture in the rock that made a perfect handhold.
ex_en: Some fractures heal stronger than before; they are the story of survival.
ex_zh: A fracture is not an ending; it is a place where new growth can begin.

quote_en: Handle the fragile glass ornaments carefully—they were your grandmother’s.
quote_zh: A fragile friendship can break with one harsh word, but it can also be mended with an apology.
ex_en: Fragile things deserve gentle hands; so do hearts.
ex_zh: What is fragile is often the most precious.

def_zh: fragment of glass; fragment of memory
quote_en: A fragment of glass was found at the crash site, the only clue to what happened.
quote_zh: The song was just a fragment of memory from her childhood, but she hummed it every day.
ex_en: From fragments, we can rebuild a whole picture.
ex_zh: Your life is a mosaic of fragments; each piece tells a part of your story.

quote_en: The frail elderly man walked with a cane, but his eyes still sparkled with mischief.
quote_zh: After the storm, there was only frail hope that the crops would survive.
ex_en: Frail bodies can hold the strongest spirits.
ex_zh: Be gentle with the frail—they have weathered storms you cannot imagine.

def_en: Sparing in use of resources; thrifty.
quote_en: A frugal lifestyle means using what you have and not wasting—like saving leftovers for tomorrow’s lunch.
quote_zh: My aunt is frugal with money so she can afford to travel each summer.
ex_en: Being frugal is not being cheap; it’s being wise with what you have.
ex_zh: A frugal heart is a generous one—it knows the value of a loaf shared.

def_en: The study of Earth’s physical structure and history.
quote_en: On our geology field trip, we hiked to see layers of rock that were millions of years old.
quote_zh: To study geology is to read the story of Earth in stone, fossil, and mountain.
ex_en: Geology teaches patience; mountains rise a grain at a time.
ex_zh: The ground beneath you holds secrets; take time to listen.

def_en: A slowly moving mass of ice.
quote_en: Scientists study glacier melt to understand how climate change affects the planet.
quote_zh: The glacier movement is so slow that it seems frozen, but it carves valleys over centuries.
ex_en: A glacier’s power is patience; it moves mountains by not rushing.
ex_zh: Some forces are quiet and slow, but they change everything.

def_en: A narrow valley with steep sides.
quote_en: The deep gorge was carved by the river over millions of years, a canyon of red rock.
quote_zh: We hiked along the river gorge, stopping to skip stones across the water.
ex_en: A gorge can seem a barrier, but it can also be a path—if you know how to cross.
ex_zh: Sometimes the deepest valleys lead to the highest peaks.

quote_en: The magician gratified the children’s wish by pulling a rabbit from his hat.
quote_zh: A good book can gratify your curiosity about faraway places and different times.
ex_en: Instant gratification feels good, but lasting satisfaction comes from effort.
ex_zh: The deepest gratification is knowing you made someone’s day brighter.

def_en: The natural home of an organism.
quote_en: The zoo tries to create a natural habitat for the elephants, with space to roam and mud to play in.
quote_zh: Habitat loss is the biggest threat to many animals when forests are cut down for cities.
ex_en: We are all caretakers of each other’s habitat.
ex_zh: Protect the habitat of the creatures you share this world with; they have no other home.

def_en: To pay attention to; to consider.
quote_en: The hikers did not heed the warning about the storm, and they got caught in the rain.
quote_zh: I should have heeded my father’s advice to bring a jacket; it got cold fast.
ex_en: To heed is to listen with the intention to act.
ex_zh: The world speaks to you in whispers; heed them before they become shouts.

def_en: Half of a sphere; half of the Earth.
quote_en: The northern hemisphere has summer while the southern hemisphere has winter—the seasons are opposite.
quote_zh: The left hemisphere of the brain is associated with logic, the right with creativity, but they work together.
ex_en: We live in one hemisphere, but our compassion can circle the whole globe.
ex_zh: Every hemisphere holds dreams; every mind holds possibilities.

def_zh: herald of spring; herald the news
quote_en: The first robin is often called the herald of spring, signaling warmer days ahead.
quote_zh: In medieval times, a herald would ride into town to announce the king’s arrival.
ex_en: Be a herald of kindness; let your actions announce that compassion is alive.
ex_zh: The smallest voice can herald the greatest change.

def_en: A symbol in a writing system; pictographic script.
quote_en: The Rosetta Stone helped scholars decipher Egyptian hieroglyphics because it had the same text in three languages.
quote_zh: Ancient hieroglyphics on tomb walls tell stories of pharaohs, gods, and everyday life.
ex_en: Hieroglyphics are frozen stories; every symbol holds a history.
ex_zh: Your life is a story; write it in symbols of kindness, courage, and truth.

quote_en: The author’s vivid imagery made me feel like I was walking through the enchanted forest.
quote_zh: Poetic imagery can turn a simple sunset into “a sky of melting apricots and gold.”
ex_en: Imagery paints pictures in the mind; choose words that build beautiful worlds.
ex_zh: The best imagery is not just seen but felt.

def_en: The effect or influence of one thing on another.
def_zh: impact on the environment; positive impact
quote_en: Recycling has a big impact on the environment by reducing waste and saving resources.
quote_zh: A kind word can have a positive impact on someone’s entire day.
ex_en: The impact of your actions ripples further than you know.
ex_zh: You can have an impact beyond your years; start small, start now.

def_en: To force upon; to establish by authority.
def_zh: impose a rule; impose on someone
quote_en: The teacher had to impose a rule about talking during tests so everyone could concentrate.
quote_zh: I didn’t want to impose on my friend by asking for a ride, but she insisted it was no trouble.
ex_en: To impose is to take without asking; to offer is to give freely.
ex_zh: Don’t impose your views; invite understanding.

def_zh: inaugurate a president; inaugurate a new bridge
quote_en: Every four years, the United States inaugurates a president with a ceremony on the Capitol steps.
quote_zh: The town will inaugurate the new bridge with a parade and fireworks.
ex_en: Every day you can inaugurate a new habit, a new way of being.
ex_zh: Begin with ceremony, but sustain with daily care.

quote_en: An industrious student finishes homework before playing, knowing that work first makes free time sweeter.
quote_zh: Bees are industrious creatures, visiting thousands of flowers to make a single jar of honey.
ex_en: Industrious hands build what idle hands only dream.
ex_zh: Be industrious in your kindness; it’s the work that never tires.

def_zh: infringe on rights; infringe a copyright
quote_en: Laws protect your right to free speech, but shouting “fire” in a crowded theater can infringe on others’ safety.
quote_zh: Copying a friend’s work without permission can infringe on their trust and your own honesty.
ex_en: Do not infringe on another’s dignity; it is their most precious possession.
ex_zh: Boundaries protect us; respect them, and others will respect yours.

quote_en: A nurse will inject the vaccine with a small needle to keep you safe from disease.
quote_zh: In the middle of the tense meeting, she managed to inject humor that made everyone relax.
ex_en: Inject hope into a situation, and watch it bloom.
ex_zh: A single kind word can inject light into a dark day.

quote_en: During theater intermission, we stretched our legs and debated who the villain really was.
quote_zh: The halftime intermission of the basketball game gave the marching band a chance to perform.
ex_en: Intermission is not an end; it’s a breath before the next act.
ex_zh: Use life’s intermissions to refuel, not to quit.

quote_en: Scientists dream of interplanetary travel that would let humans visit Mars and beyond.
quote_zh: The interplanetary probe Voyager has left our solar system, carrying a golden record with sounds of Earth.
ex_en: Interplanetary distances remind us how small our world is—and how precious.
ex_zh: Reach across the interplanetary space of misunderstanding with a message of peace.

def_zh: intersect at a point; paths intersect
quote_en: In geometry, two lines intersect at a point if they are not parallel.
quote_zh: Our paths intersected at summer camp, and we’ve been friends ever since.
ex_en: Where dreams and effort intersect, success begins.
ex_zh: The best moments happen when curiosity and courage intersect.

quote_en: The lighthouse stood on an isolated island, reachable only by boat in calm weather.
quote_zh: When you move to a new town, it’s easy to feel isolated until you make friends who welcome you.
ex_en: Even in the most isolated place, you are never truly alone; the stars keep watch.
ex_zh: Isolation can teach you to know yourself; solitude is a garden where inner strength grows.
